author: | Thor | [STAR]
content:
Good evening I hope INK is flowing for everyone, I would like to loop back to a discussion of repair and rescue game loop in C4 and beyond. I did not hear anything specifics stated in the town halls so I would like to present a concept for thought. I also created a more extensive draft in PIP proposal form but am not sure where the proper place for discussion on this game development would be:
Rescue Mechanics—

Each crewmember is given a ransom certificate of insurance whenever they are added to a ship the cost of certificate of insurance is paid in LP and is required for every crew member and passenger. The cost of the certificate of insurance is factored based off of the rarity of the crewmember , this provides a non-treatable asset cost and provides LP with Utility. If a ship were to be destroyed, based off of the escape pods on each ship, the certificate of insurance would be represented as the “cargo lost“ instead of the actual crewmember, allowing for easier on chain logic, while maintaining the conceptual equivalence of crewmembers, lost from the destruction of a ship, providing a penalty, making ship destruction, more undesirable and they benefit, encouraging the aggressor to target ships fitting into the war economy of Star Atlas. 
Let me know if this is similar to what is planned or if this is a concept worth developing and discussing more… cuz I have more details

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: I think that server-side automation is a band-aid solution, and somewhat counter to the ethos of a fully on-chain game. Now, if "server-side" actually refers to an on-chain solution, then that could be interesting.  However, my question was really - would you consider a redesign of the game mechanics such that the need for automation of any kind is eliminated (or, more likely, significantly reduced)?  I understand that any type of redesign will have side effects, but as an example - if a mining fleet was "connected" to the starbase inventory, that fleet could mine for as long as resources/space on the starbase was available without the need to stop/dock/unload/reload/undock/start again. There would be very little benefit in automating the mining loop in this scenario.

author: [AEP] Viktor [AEP]
content:
@bunthius 
Hey Danny,
I have a few questions about the SAGE Editor Suite.

1) What is the default or base config?
The first question concerns the default or base configuration in the ship configurator. I thought the default or base config was tier 0. Let's take the Fimbul Airbike, for example. The ship configurator shows a base value of 249 for cargo_capacity. By selecting the Tier One ship config, this value increases to 295.81. If you click on it, you can see that the increase comes from the tractor beam, which is now Tier 1.
Isn't the Airbike's tractor beam already at tier 1, and doesn't the 249 already account for this Tier 1 component? I was expecting a lower base value for cargo capacity for the base (Tier 0), which increases to 249 with the upgrade (Tier 1).

2) Do we get all ship components and modules from the ship parts so we can use them for ship configs?

3) I really like the ship components in the menu because you can see the effects of upgrades there. They also seem to be the most reliable (see question 4). However, there are some values ​​that are hard to believe. For example, the heat sink. Does an upgrade really result in a 50% reduction for the scan_cool_down and the repair_cool_down? Of course, that makes sense for a heat sink, but compared to the scanner array upgrade, which offers a 20% reduction for the scan_cool_down, it seems a lot.

4) Comparing the results from the ship comparison table with the combined formula of the stat details, there seem to be significant differences. For example, let's apply the "Scanning Max" ship config upgrade to the Fimbul Airbike. The table shows that the subwarp speed is increased by 877% to 0.0821. If you click on this value, you can see that the combined formula only shows an increase of 89.1%, which seems much more reasonable.

However, if you look at the scan_cool_down, the table shows an increase of 186% to 2,248 seconds, which doesn't make sense, as this stat should be going down with the upgrade. If you click on it to view the stat details, the combined formula does show a decrease in the scan_cool_down, which seems more reasonable, but it is turning negative. Something seems to be wrong here.

5) Chromite ore is missing from the Filter tab of the map editor.

6) The costs shown for the building in the Claim Stakes tools are just placeholders, right? They are very low and very similar if you compare the buildings. Like 25 arco, 20 frameworks and 15 copper ore. 

The SAGE Editor Suite is an amazing set of tools and I really love that you decided to publish it, I just want to make sure that our plans for C4 make sense.

author: [AEP] Viktor [AEP]
content: Those are base stats ported over from Starbased, i've reset the idea that that there is any associated tier and this is the new baseline. It's not a proven strategy yet but it's the mode I'm building this effort on. Because It's a cleaner experience to just flatly upgrade tier of all the slotted equipment instead of giving varying upgrades of different pieces of gear per major config. So, if it does prove to be problematic I just have to reduce the out of scale stats and this system still holds up. I just haven't done that yet.,Yes, but my plan at the moment is to just give full tier 1 or tier 2 components/modules based on the perceived value that was baked into the original purchase. Instead of varying tiers per items.,Totally okay to ignore the effects as they're all placeholder atm. So questioning consistency isn't very useful at the moment and I can't jump in right now to fine tune in response as I'm fussing with other tasks atm. I will be setting up a feedback channel when i'm able to more dutifully react.,will take a look when i can get back to it. possibly related to a stray tier scaling formula or the improper buffing effect as noting in another post. am aware of the negative stats where they shouldn't be. the tool allows for adjustments for those to fall in line, just haven't gone through this secondary phase yet from placeholder to meticulous inspecting of the effects of everything.,Thanks for the heads up, we're doing another map pass soon as quite a bit will need to change in how resources are distributed and a couple of missing items as you noted.,Yes, def placeholder, I would basically put zero credibility to amounts of any type as that requires a full econ pass anyway so the goal here was just to make things functional., I like that you're able to see what we see. But basically until we go live everything will consistently be in flux, I've never worked so publicly or even heard of any team working so publicly with live internal tooling and stats for a game. It's a very unusual development step. Even internally we usually don't share this openly until it's ready for direct feedback. So, it's an interesting experiment, hope everyone can enjoy it for what it is.

author: Odicerous
content:
Great Town Hall today, thank you!  Very excited for everything you guys have cooking.

During the TH, I had  a thought about ship configs.What if they constantly degraded and required replacement?  Very slowly for ships with light or no use, and linearly increasing for ships with high use and suffering combat damage.
Just spitballing, but maybe making ship configs easier to craft but shorter lived would promote experimentation, and keep the resource sink growing and never satisfied? 

Players might be incentivized to try different combinations when they reload, because its just temporary.  Upgrading more often might lead to new crafting loops.  Unused ships would suffer some depreciation, encouraging use or rentals.

Hopefully, players' fleets would remain dynamic, and never get to that "set and forget" mode.Whales would need to constantly source new configs, ideally from community suppliers.And instead of a "junk pile" of dead resources from configs nobody wants anymore, its a never ending, renewable Atlas sink.

author: ROME | Saint-JustPirate Y'aaar
content: I'm not totally opposed to this concept. It's used in many other MMOs, and was the basis of the economy in Entropia. It does help keep the economy churning. And our plan is already low value/high turnover for component crafting, particularly considering their use as a sink in C4 Combat. Though there's still some massaging happening at the higher tier components.  That said, mechanics like this also introduce a grind that isn't precisely fun. Kind of like earlier conversations we've had about crew eventually "dying," and needing to be replaced through some procreation process. Interesting to consider, but not sure we'd move in that direction.

Interesting to consider, but not sure we'd move in that direction.

author: [Λ] Sawyn [BR] [STAR]
content:
@bunthius or @Deleted User Since we have different levels of players in Unreal, a player ranking system would be interesting. There's a lot of imbalance between players.

I'm not saying that only strong players should play against strong players. Let's analyze how, in any RPG's XP system, eliminating a mob 10x better than you will give you 10x more XP. Likewise, eliminating a starting mob at level 200 will give you almost no XP.

To help and encourage strong players to keep playing, define a tier, and the higher the tier, the greater the bonus at the end of each period (it could be weekly or monthly).

So, even if the strong player doesn't get much XP from a single kill, they will get a lot due to the total amount of XP in the match (as it is now), and the low-end player will get more than 100 XP in a 10-minute match.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Influence had real world decay timers and it was an absolute disaster.  You had to lease your land from the planet owner (game devs) and if your lease ever lapsed you lost all of your stuff to other players when they took the lease.  And most players were doing 30 day leases.  So if you missed the lease by 1 day, poof, you just lost everything.  Or, if you took a break, you could never return.   That was biggest negative for me. You could NEVER have returning players because all of their assets were gone and they would have to rebuy everything.  Which no player would ever do.  This of course was all hindsight, no one realized the repercussions.  Lots of players enjoyed stealing all of the players assets who left.  But then, all the players were gone.  And well, what's the point of being a King if you have no subjects.

author: Odicerous
content:
I agree with all of this.  To clarify, I was not suggesting that any core assets decay, especially not ships or structures or crew.  I hate that idea.  And I am not a fan of crew aging either.  Not OK with real world assets.

Excepting of course, asset loss in the HRZ, I am all for that!  But that is 100% assumed risk/reward, and very different.

I just meant the lesser add-ons.  The configurables that allow optimization, but are not necessary to play the game.  If your super shield add-on, or big ass proton gun, or warp engine supercharger had a limited life span, it could sort of act like an extension of R4, needing replenishment, but over a much longer lifespan, and with different configuration benefits. 

I wonder if this might encourage more creative game play, more customization, and more diversified crafting loops.It sounds like this was already considered.

author: [SAI] Urto666
content: I can address this for you. This was a very targeted moderation effort in the game. Last night I was witness to nearly two dozen fleets hit-trading for combat XP across three starbases -- MRZ-26/27/31. The "NPC" fleets you captured in your images were used to directly target and destroy fleets participating in that exploitive behavior. In doing so, it was necessary to capture the associated starbases to prevent them from simply docking their fleets to escape the attack. Two starbases, 27 and 28 (an escape route) were captured for this purpose. Those fleets were subsequently left on patrol at those starbases as several fleets participating in this behavior remained docked at those SBs, and I didn't want to see them re-engage in that activity as soon as I was offline. With the exception of MRZ-31, those SBs are not really used for mining, but specifically for this hit-trade activity. To be clear, this was not a "raid," it wasn't about fun, and it wasn't about re-establishing balance between factions, which are how these fleets have been employed in the past. It was a preventative measure.  If you were trying to mine MRZ-31 when you logged in, then I apologize, you were likely collateral damage from this moderation effort.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
I’m writing here instead of on SACK because I think it’s fair that others can challenge my ideas if needed. I don’t claim to have a crystal ball, even if I tend to have a sharp eye for the gaming industry, so I’d like some healthy debate in case someone doesnt agree.

I think the approach towards the UE5 module has been excellent: regular major updates, constant hotfixes, and careful attention to optimization with each iteration. Kudos to Chip and the entire dev team!

That said, I think enough time has passed to analyze user behavior with UE. In my opinion, we really need a PvE or PvPvE mode where players can farm XP without always being forced to fight others. Right now, there are very few “true” gamers in the community—barely a handful. Most members are investors or casual players. This leads to a negative cycle: casual players get “farmed to death” by the strong ones, quit after a few tries because they gain no XP by just dying, then the strong players end up in empty lobbies with no progress to make, and they quit too. Same goes for races—people prefer to play alone rather than with others, since finishing second cuts XP almost in half.

The sneak peek of bots on Paizul made me really happy, since it goes in this direction, but I fear they’ll only be available in tutorial lobbies. Leaving Galia aside (I’d discuss that separately if anyone’s interested), I think it would be interesting to make the Gunplay Trainer multiplayer, or to add bots to Arena lobbies. It might be worth checking with Chris whether this is sustainable in terms of INK.

I see two possible approaches:

PvE mode: 1 team of 4–6 players against 10–12 bots (with reduced XP compared to the 100 points of “real” players).

PvPvE mode: 3 teams of 3 players each (FFA), plus a bot team of 9 (normal XP for killing players, reduced XP for bots).

The downside is more servers being active (higher cost), but the upside is that many more people will be encouraged to play. This could significantly increase the player base, who won’t feel like cannon fodder anymore and will be able to play in the style they prefer.

All of this would require minimal extra dev work (since maps and logic already exist). The main challenge is bot AI—especially vertical flying as Chip said multiple times—but that could be balanced by awarding low XP at first, then scaling it up as the bots improve. Difficulty levels could also be added to lobbies (set by the lobby creator on dedicated servers), with XP scaling based on difficulty.

author: Chri.z [STAR]
content:
RE discussion in FR-Chat:

I try not to get involved in the discussion too much these days as I want to keep an objective and unbiased view when tweaking the economy. But this is a major update and I want you all to know that I'm listening.  Like MW, I do lack a bit of sympathy to many of the points brought up here. But much of that lack of sympathy is due to my not being able to understand what it is some of you are upset about. I see that you don't like the new LP values but I feel there is insufficient explanation for why (more on this later). Similarly, I see many people don't like the Phantom Starbases and, again, I haven't seen a logical reason why (one that goes beyond, "it's not fair"). 

The only sober explanation I can glean from the discussion is that the shock is too big. Now this is a point that I am taking very seriously. My original numbers have already been dampened by a 50% modifier (effect sizes have all been cut in half). After seeing the discussion and fear in the community here, I'm returning to my analysis to dig even deeper to see if a 25% damper is more appropriate. The team feels 50% is sufficient but we always strive to be better and we use feedback as a valuable input.

The lesson here is that while feedback is always welcome, it's not always helpful. If I don't have actionable explanations for why something is bad or good, it's impossible to study it with my data. "I don't like it" and "It's not fair" aren't helpful. But "This will shock the commodity markets and send Iron Ore soaring while killing Silica" is a much more actionable insight. I can actually go and study that statement to see if it holds water. Anyway, I need to get to work so I'll let you guys have at it. Thanks!

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: The current problem statement with the phantom base available is that: all components can be installed in unlimited quantities.  Of course 1-2 components will be better to install then the other. As a result only those components get installed on phantom, resulting in a huge dilution spike for the faction, making all other components negative to very very negative returns to install.   The current upcoming changes don't change that much it simply moved the incentive from installing Power source and Field stabilizer to a future state of installing only Frameworks and SDU.  The boost in the value of framework makes it good to install even at 75 bil to 100 bil LP while PS at the same level is earning 25% of its market value leading to a loss of 75% of value. It rewards extractors and drives builders to quit the game / game loop.    The most simple and logical solution without much change would have been a 50% dampener on installs done on Phantom base   What would that achieve ? for days where there are other bases to install components at it would penalize those installing at phantom., after all bases are done it would give LP based on phantom bases output.  From: AEP Ustur faction.

author: Kr1gs
content:
Happy Saturday @bunthius !

Couple of questions regarding the SES and resources and a mechanic.  Given that the data in SES is very likely to change, wanted to gauge its current state regarding the following:

Resource distribution and layout.  I can't imagine you guys manually input resources in spots one by one and there is a certain algorithm to it for both density and richness.  Is the current resource layout in the SES subject to change heavily, or is it in its near final state? I only ask cause it actually seems that there aren't too many faction restricted resources, and the dispersion of resources seems fairly equal between the MRZ and SRZ, albeit in varying levels of richness.  Additionally, the faction resources of Diamond, Arco, Rorchinol - in the SES, only Diamond is faction specific whereas Arco and Rorchinol become map wide and incredibly less rare in C4.,
Planet mining vs Asteroid mining - Given the mechanic of planet mining vs asteroid mining, it was noted that nearly all (like 90%) of all raw resources are found on planets where only a handful (13 or some) are located in undesignated asteroid belts.  This would subsequently imply that a significant portion of mining is to be done via claim stakes.  Will planetary mining only be done by claimstakes as has been stated in the past? Is that the intended mechanic claimstake vs ship mining i.e 90:10%? In short terms, it seems like Armstrong ships (and technically all ships) will be limited in what they can mine.  Wanted to get a better understanding of mining limitation both from a theorycrafting standpoint but also a portfolio balancing standpoint.  Disclosure: I love mining.,
Thanks.  Doesnt have to be complicated answers, I believe I asked fairly straightforward questions.

author: [DEEP] Digital Fists
content:
May I make a suggestion that if more recipes are added to crafting, with very high resource requirements, to broaded that particular game loop, that there is more of a focus on offering something novel or exclusive as a reward for people's efforts, rather than an existing ship? No one in their right mind is going to waste over $2k worth of resources for a Mamba Ex that you can buy for $200. Just sell the resources you've worked for and buy 10.

I understand the idea of offering a crafting game loop for people to engage in who don't necessarily want to just focus on profit, but I think it would be better to offer even a useless badge or a cosmetic item, or even better an exclusive crew pack, anything with at least some exclusivity value or prestige, rather than a ship you can easily buy for a tenth of the price.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
Hey, team. (Especially @Chri.z for this one).

1st: Crafting fees reduction is  !

Now, Phantom Base. Seems like its existence is irrevocable. And I get it. Sage needs an endless sink, with little dev time devoted to it. But it also became the meta. Not the LP values itself, but the Phantom Base itself. And from all comments I've read, and all messages I received from other players, that seems to be the core issue. I've been putting a lot of thought into the whole PB situation, and how an easy fix for it could be implemented, without needing a lot of Dev time. It's a hard one, because the very nature of it makes it super attractive, thus creating a lot of extracting pressure (people will go for the low hanging fruit, it's human nature).

What if the PB was repositioned? Much (MUCH) further into the MRZ/HRZ? This would retain its function (an endless sink for Sage, while not increasing inflation - like FICs). But would also remove the core issue that it introduced: being the easiest of all loops. Making the hardest to reach would actually favor actual gameplay, and the base would still be there for when bases are finished. We'd still have issues with SDU installing (which could perhaps be tackled by bumping SDUs LP value up?). But pairing it with PB being super far would (probably) remove the incentive to just dump it all into PB.

Either way, this is all I can think of. Not even sure if I should meddle into this discussion further, but it just made sense to share this thought.

Thanks for all the hardwork, and I can only imagine the pressure you guys are dealing with right now. It's a really rough moment in macro, and it's impossible to not feel it bleeding into SA.

author: [NEON] AlphaPunk [NEON]
content:
Hey team,the NEON Army eSports Team is planning to host regular cross-community tournaments/events.Our first event will be in November, starting with a Star Atlas Racing tournament.We are funding a 4-figure prize pool and will also be looking for sponsors.
Before moving forward with marketing and sponsorships, we want to share critical feedback from our players regarding latency and server performance:
"This seems to be more of a server tick rate issue.During the pre-race countdown, every boost input works perfectly.Once the race begins, however, the NA and EU servers only register about every second boost, dropping inputs and preventing maximum speed.On the Asia and SA servers, boosts work as intended, leading to a race time difference of about 15 seconds."

---

 Why this is important:For an event with a 4-figure prize pool (and larger pools in the future), outcomes should not depend on server luck. Fair conditions are essential for competitive integrity.
---

 Our questions:Are there any planned optimizations for racing/shooter servers before November?,Could we be allowed to run a dedicated server?It would be available not only to NEON Army members but also to other Germany/Europe players (up to a certain player cap).,,

It's a hard one, because the very nature of it makes it super attractive, thus creating a lot of extracting pressure (people will go for the low hanging fruit, it's human nature).  What if the PB was repositioned? Much (MUCH) further into the MRZ/HRZ? This would retain its function (an endless sink for Sage, while not increasing inflation - like FICs). But would also remove the core issue that it introduced: being the easiest of all loops. Making the hardest to reach would actually favor actual gameplay, and the base would still be there for when bases are finished. We'd still have issues with SDU installing (which could perhaps be tackled by bumping SDUs LP value up?). But pairing it with PB being super far would (probably) remove the incentive to just dump it all into PB.  Either way, this is all I can think of. Not even sure if I should meddle into this discussion further, but it just made sense to share this thought.  Thanks for all the hardwork, and I can only imagine the pressure you guys are dealing with right now. It's a really rough moment in macro, and it's impossible to not feel it bleeding into SA.

author: Lysimach | Make Polis GreatMUD
content: Appreciate this feedback very much! This isn't a bad suggestion at all. Couple of notes to think about: We did position them with smaller fleets in mind. We wanted to make sure it was feasible for everyone to get involved. But I certainly see the point that they are maybe too easy to access. That said, I don't know if making them farther away solves the "max extraction" problem. Feels like a regular shake up would do a better job at disrupting the max extraction problem, no? Furthermore, we would have to completely shut these current bases down (can't move them) and instead create new bases further out. Seems a shame to lose the current starbases entirely imo.
